---
title: "Graph geometry: meshes, graph Laplacians, and spectra"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
A triangle mesh is also a *graph* — vertices are nodes, mesh edges are graph
edges — and a surprising amount of surface geometry has an exact graph-theory
twin. This page draws those correspondences. The **graph (combinatorial)
Laplacian** $L_{\text{graph}}=D-A$ uses only *who-neighbours-whom*; the
[**cotangent Laplacian**](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) adds the *metric*
(triangle shapes) and is the one that converges to the smooth
Laplace–Beltrami operator. Same sparsity pattern, different weights. From there
the eigenvectors of one are the **graph-Fourier basis** (spectral clustering,
Laplacian eigenmaps) and the eigenfunctions of the other are
[**manifold harmonics**](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen) — same machinery, different
operator. We then read the [k-ring adjacency](operators.qmd#sec-adjacency) as a
graph signal-processing operator, cover **mesh pooling / hierarchies** for
surface encoder–decoders (which turn out to be nothing more than [semiring matmuls](operators.qmd#sec-semiring)
across resolution levels), and point onward to the federation's connectivity and
parcellation work. This is a reference/bridge page: it connects the geometry unit
to graph theory rather than re-deriving either.
:::

## A motivating question: is this a surface or a network?

Take a cortical surface mesh — the white-matter boundary of one hemisphere,
roughly $n \approx 164{,}000$ vertices for FreeSurfer's `ico7`. You can ask two
families of question about it, and they *look* identical but are not:

- **Geometric.** How curved is the cortex here? How far apart, *along the
  surface*, are two points? What does the surface's vibration spectrum look
  like? These depend on the *metric* — actual lengths, angles, areas.
- **Combinatorial.** Which vertices are connected to which? How many hops from
  here to there? What communities of densely-interconnected vertices exist?
  These depend only on the *connectivity* — the graph, with no notion of length.

The mesh carries both. The face list $F$ defines a graph $(V,E)$: an edge $(i,j)$
exists whenever some triangle contains both $i$ and $j$. The vertex coordinates
$V\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times3}$ add the metric on top. Almost every operator in this
unit lives at one of these two altitudes, and the single most useful thing this
page can teach you is **how to tell which altitude an operator is at** — because
choosing the metric-blind tool for a metric question is the most common way to
get a plausible-looking but geometrically wrong answer.

The unit's governing idea applies here in a sharpened form. A discrete mesh
approximates a smooth surface; the *metric-aware* discrete operators (cotangent
Laplacian, Voronoi mass) are built precisely so that they **converge to the
continuous truth under refinement** and pass analytic-oracle checks (a unit
sphere's mean curvature $H=1/r=1$). The *metric-blind* graph operators do not
converge to anything geometric — and that is not a bug, it is the point: they
answer connectivity questions, which have no continuous-geometry limit to
converge to.

---

## Mesh = graph + metric {#sec-graph-laplacian}

### The graph Laplacian: connectivity only

Start with the simplest Laplacian a reader fluent in linear algebra would
invent. You have an adjacency matrix $A$, with $A_{ij}=1$ when $i$ and $j$ are
neighbours and $0$ otherwise, and a diagonal **degree** matrix
$D=\operatorname{diag}(d_i)$ with $d_i=\sum_j A_{ij}$ the number of neighbours of
vertex $i$. The **combinatorial graph Laplacian** is

$$
L_{\text{graph}} = D - A,
\qquad (L_{\text{graph}}\,u)_i = \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} \bigl(u_i - u_j\bigr).
$$ {#eq-graph-laplacian}

Read the right-hand form aloud: *at every vertex, sum the differences between my
value and each neighbour's value.* That is a discrete "how much do I stick out
from my neighbourhood" operator. It is symmetric and positive-semidefinite, its
smallest eigenvalue is $0$ (with the constant eigenvector — a flat signal sticks
out from nobody), and its row sums are zero. This is exactly the structure of a
Laplacian, built from connectivity alone. Crucially, **every neighbour counts
equally**: the operator never looks at where the vertices actually are.

`nitrix` builds this in `nitrix.graph.laplacian.laplacian(A, normalisation=...)`,
a **dense** constructor (**HOST-CTOR**: it materialises an $(n,n)$ matrix; the
symmetric variant's diagonal is generally non-zero so it does not fit the sparse
pattern of the input). Three normalisations are offered, and each is one rung up
from the last:

- `'combinatorial'`: $L = D - A$ exactly as above. Eigenvalues are unbounded
  (they scale with degree).
- `'symmetric'`: $L_{\text{sym}} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$. The obvious problem
  with the combinatorial form is that high-degree vertices dominate; the cure is
  to divide out degree symmetrically. The result is symmetric, PSD, with
  eigenvalues in $[0,2]$ — the canonical *normalised Laplacian* used in
  Laplacian-eigenmap embeddings.
- `'random_walk'`: $L_{\text{rw}} = I - D^{-1} A$. Here $D^{-1}A$ is the
  row-stochastic transition matrix of a random walk on the graph, so this
  Laplacian is the *generator* of that walk. It is not symmetric in general but
  shares the spectrum of $L_{\text{sym}}$.

For graphs too large for a dense $(n,n)$ matrix, the page you want is the
**matrix-free** sibling `laplacian_matvec(A, x, normalisation=..., promise_symmetry=...)`
(**DIFF-JAX**): it applies $L\,x$ without ever forming $L$, accepting dense, `ELL`,
or `SectionedELL` adjacency (see [the ELL formats](operators.qmd#sec-ell)). This
is the operator handed to the iterative eigensolvers in [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve).
The `promise_symmetry` flag is the same opt-in you meet throughout the sparse
stack: with it `False` (safe for asymmetric stored patterns such as a top-$k$
sparsified affinity) the matvec applies the symmetric part $\tfrac12(Ax+A^{\!\top}x)$;
with it `True` it applies the bare $Ax$ and skips the adjoint pass.

The supporting degree machinery is public and worth knowing by name, because the
distinction it encodes is exactly the "symmetrise the adjacency, not the
Laplacian" trap that bites people building affinities:

- `degree_vector(A)` — the **out-degree** (row sum) $\sum_j A_{ij}$. For ELL it
  is `A.values.sum(-1)`; for `SectionedELL` it sums each bucket and scatters back
  to row order. (**DIFF-JAX**.)
- `in_degree_vector(A)` — the **in-degree** (column sum) $\sum_i A_{ij}$, the
  adjoint of the above, computed without materialising $A^{\!\top}$ (dense
  `A.sum(-2)`; sparse via the additive ELL adjoint $A^{\!\top}\mathbf{1}$). Equal
  to the out-degree iff $A$ is symmetric. (**DIFF-JAX**.)
- `symmetric_degree_vector(A)` — the degree of the *symmetrised* adjacency
  $W=\tfrac12(A+A^{\!\top})$, i.e. $d_i=\tfrac12(\text{out}_i+\text{in}_i)$. This
  is the degree you must normalise by when $A$ is not symmetric; using the bare
  out-degree there normalises by the wrong diagonal and drifts the trivial
  (constant) eigenvalue off $1$. (**DIFF-JAX**.)

### The cotangent Laplacian: connectivity plus metric

Now climb one rung. The graph Laplacian's flaw, *for a geometry question*, is
that two meshes with identical connectivity but wildly different vertex positions
get identical operators — it cannot tell a sphere from a crumpled version of the
same triangulation. The cure is to weight each edge by the *shape* of the
triangles around it. That is exactly the
[**cotangent Laplacian**](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent), built by
`mesh_cotangent_laplacian(mesh, format=...)` (**HOST-CTOR**, returns `ELL`/`SectionedELL`):

$$
(L\,u)_i = \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij}\,(u_j - u_i),
\qquad w_{ij} = \tfrac12\bigl(\cot\alpha_{ij} + \cot\beta_{ij}\bigr),
$$ {#eq-cotangent}

where $\alpha_{ij},\beta_{ij}$ are the two angles *opposite* the edge $(i,j)$ in
the triangles sharing it (one cotangent for a boundary edge in an open mesh). The
sparsity pattern of @eq-cotangent is **identical** to that of @eq-graph-laplacian
— same neighbour set $\mathcal{N}(i)$, same nonzeros — but the weights now encode
the metric. Set every $w_{ij}=1$ and you recover the graph Laplacian (the
"umbrella" / uniform operator). Let the cotangents vary with triangle shape and
you get the operator that, divided by the [Voronoi mass](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-mass)
$M=\operatorname{diag}(A_i)$, gives the discrete Laplace–Beltrami operator
$\Delta = M^{-1}L$ that converges to the smooth one under refinement.

@fig-graph-geometry-weights makes the difference concrete on an analytic
triangle fan: the graph Laplacian assigns weight $1$ to every spoke regardless of
geometry, while the cotangent weights respond to triangle shape — collapsing
toward zero (and even going negative) as a triangle becomes obtuse and skinny.

```{python}
#| label: fig-graph-geometry-weights
#| fig-cap: "Same sparsity pattern, different weights. An interior vertex (red) in a triangle fan: the graph (combinatorial) Laplacian weights every spoke equally (w=1), while the cotangent weights w_0j respond to triangle shape. Skewing one ring vertex outward (right) makes its triangles long and skinny, driving those cotangent weights down — the metric speaking."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Analytic mesh patch: interior vertex 0 + a 1-ring of m boundary vertices,
# a triangle fan. We control triangle shape (hence cotangent weights) by
# where the ring vertices sit.
m = 6
ang = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, m, endpoint=False)
ring = np.stack([np.cos(ang), np.sin(ang)], axis=1)
center = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
ring_skew = ring.copy()
ring_skew[1] *= 2.4  # push one vertex out -> long skinny triangles

def cot(u, v):
    dot = u @ v
    crs = u[0] * v[1] - u[1] * v[0]
    return dot / (abs(crs) + 1e-30)

def fan_weights(ring_xy):
    # Cotangent weight w_0j for each spoke (vertex 0 -> ring vertex j):
    # w = 1/2 (cot a + cot b), with a, b the angles opposite the spoke edge
    # in the two triangles incident to it.
    P = np.vstack([center, ring_xy])
    w = np.zeros(m)
    for j in range(m):
        jp, jn, jb = 1 + j, 1 + (j + 1) % m, 1 + (j - 1) % m
        a = cot(P[0] - P[jn], P[jp] - P[jn])
        b = cot(P[0] - P[jb], P[jp] - P[jb])
        w[j] = 0.5 * (a + b)
    return w

w_reg, w_skew = fan_weights(ring), fan_weights(ring_skew)

fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.6))
for ax, ring_xy, w, title in (
    (axs[0], ring, w_reg, "regular fan"),
    (axs[1], ring_skew, w_skew, "skewed fan (one long triangle)"),
):
    P = np.vstack([center, ring_xy])
    for j in range(m):
        tri = np.array([P[0], P[1 + j], P[1 + (j + 1) % m], P[0]])
        ax.plot(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], color="0.6", lw=0.8, zorder=1)
    wn = w / np.max(np.abs(w))
    for j in range(m):
        seg = np.array([P[0], P[1 + j]])
        ax.plot(
            seg[:, 0], seg[:, 1],
            color=plt.cm.viridis(0.15 + 0.8 * np.clip(wn[j], 0, 1)),
            lw=1 + 5 * np.clip(wn[j], 0, 1), solid_capstyle="round", zorder=2,
            label="cotangent $w_{0j}$" if j == 0 else None,
        )
    ax.scatter(P[1:, 0], P[1:, 1], s=40, color="0.2", zorder=3)
    ax.scatter([0], [0], s=80, color="crimson", zorder=4, label="interior vertex 0")
    ax.set_title(f"{title}\ngraph $w$=1 (uniform); cotangent $w$ varies")
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off"); ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Same sparsity pattern, different weights: graph vs cotangent Laplacian", y=1.02)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Which one, when

The choice is not a matter of taste; it follows from the question:

| Use the **graph** Laplacian when… | Use the **cotangent** Laplacian when… |
|---|---|
| the answer depends only on topology / connectivity | the answer depends on lengths, angles, areas |
| you want cheap, robust, mesh-resolution-independent behaviour | you need convergence to the smooth $\Delta$ |
| coordinates are unreliable or absent (pure connectome) | the mesh faithfully samples a real surface |
| e.g. uniform vertex smoothing, fold detection, GSP filters | e.g. mean curvature, geodesic smoothing, manifold harmonics |

The pure-connectivity workhorse is `mesh_laplacian_smooth(vertices, faces, lam=, iterations=)`
(**DIFF-JAX**): one *uniform-Laplacian* (umbrella) smoothing pass moves every
vertex a fraction $\lambda$ toward the **unweighted mean of its 1-ring**,
$v \leftarrow (1-\lambda)v + \lambda\,\overline{\mathcal{N}(v)}$, iterated inside a
`jax.lax.fori_loop`. It is metric-blind by design — it shrinks and regularises a
mesh using connectivity only, and because it never calls a host constructor in
the loop body it is safe to jit (it builds the edge index arrays once, outside
the loop). Contrast it with the metric-aware backward-Euler geodesic smoothing
$(M + tL)x = Mx_0$ in [smoothing](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-smoothing), which uses
the cotangent $L$ and Voronoi mass $M$ and therefore *does* respect surface
distances.

A note on the **random-walk / normalised** form at the mesh level: the k-ring
adjacency constructor (next section) can return a **row-stochastic** operator
directly (`binary=False`), giving you $D^{-1}A$ — neighbourhood *averaging*
rather than summing — without a separate normalisation step. That is the discrete
random walk on the mesh graph, and the natural diffusion / smoothing kernel when
you want a metric-blind low-pass filter.

---

## Spectral graph theory ↔ manifold harmonics {#sec-spectral-graph}

Here the twin structure pays off most. Eigendecompose either Laplacian and the
eigenvectors form an orthogonal basis ordered by "frequency" (eigenvalue): low
eigenvalues are smooth, slowly-varying patterns; high eigenvalues are wiggly. For
the **graph** Laplacian this basis is the **graph-Fourier basis** — the
foundation of spectral clustering, Laplacian eigenmaps (Belkin & Niyogi 2003),
and graph signal processing. For the **cotangent** Laplacian it is the basis of
**manifold harmonics** — the discrete analogue of the vibration modes (spherical
harmonics on a sphere) covered in [spectral](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen). *Same
machinery, different operator.* The generalised eigenproblem $L\phi_k=\lambda_k M\phi_k$
is solved the same way regardless of which $L,M$ you feed it; what makes the
result geometric is the operator, not the solver.

### Laplacian eigenmaps in `nitrix.graph.connectopy`

`nitrix` ships the graph-spectral side as `laplacian_eigenmap` (**DIFF-JAX** — the
solver carries an implicit-VJP, see below) in `nitrix.graph.connectopy`. It embeds
nodes in the **bottom-$k$ non-trivial** eigenspace of the **symmetric normalised**
Laplacian $L = I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$. Two implementation choices in the real
source are worth teaching because they are easy to get wrong:

1. **It chases the *largest* eigenvalues of the affinity, not the smallest of the
   Laplacian.** The interesting eigenvectors of $L$ are at the *bottom* of its
   spectrum (smoothest), but iterative solvers are far happier chasing the *top*.
   The code uses the identity $L = I - M$ with $M = D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$ the
   normalised affinity, so $\lambda_L = 1-\lambda_M$: the bottom of $L$ is the top
   of $M$. It eigendecomposes $M$ for its largest eigenpairs (via
   `eigsolve_top_k` in `nitrix.linalg._eigsolve` — the same dispatcher the
   geometry suite's sphere eigensolve uses), drops the trivial leading constant
   eigenvector (`skip_trivial=True`), maps $\lambda_L = 1-\lambda_M$, and re-sorts
   smallest-first.
2. **It symmetrises correctly.** Affinities from top-$k$-per-row sparsification
   are generally *not* symmetric. With `promise_symmetry=False` (default) the
   operator is symmetrised at the matvec to $\tfrac12(Ax+A^{\!\top}x)$ *and* the
   normalisation uses `symmetric_degree_vector` $d_W=\tfrac12(\text{out}+\text{in})$,
   so the trivial eigenvalue stays exactly at $1$. Set `promise_symmetry=True`
   only when the adjacency is genuinely symmetric (regular meshes / grids), which
   skips the adjoint passes (~2× cheaper).

The solver is selected by `solver=` (`'auto'`, `'eigh'`, `'lobpcg'`,
`'shift_invert'`) and `preconditioner=` (`'none'`, `'polynomial'`), which map onto
a `SolverSpec` for the [eigensolve dispatcher](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve);
`'auto'` resolves to dense `eigh` for dense input and `lobpcg` for sparse.
Differentiability comes from `jnp.linalg.eigh`'s registered VJP (dense `eigh`
path) or the implicit-VJP in `linalg._eigsolve` (iterative paths). Inputs may be
dense, `ELL`, or `SectionedELL`. The companion `diffusion_embedding`
(Coifman–Lafon 2006, **DIFF-JAX**) reuses the same affinity-eigensolve plumbing
with a density-normalisation parameter `alpha` and a diffusion-time scaling
$\lambda^t$, recovering the random-walk right eigenvectors $\psi = D^{-1/2}\phi$.

### The as-built caveat: eigenmaps are the *wrong* operator for a geometric sphere embedding

This is the load-bearing lesson of the page, and it is a real design decision in
the source, not a hypothetical. `laplacian_eigenmap` builds a **degree-normalised
graph Laplacian** $I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$. That is a *graph* operator: it knows
who-neighbours-whom and how many, but it does **not** know triangle areas or
angles. It is therefore the **wrong** operator if what you want is a *geometric*
spectral embedding of a surface — the kind that should reproduce a sphere's
low-order spherical harmonics. The geometry-correct object is the cotangent-mass
generalised eigenproblem $L\phi_k = \lambda_k M\phi_k$ with the **cotangent**
stiffness and the **Voronoi mass** — not the degree-normalised graph Laplacian.

That is exactly why the surface spectral embedding in the geometry suite does
**not** route through `laplacian_eigenmap`: the [spectral](spectral.qmd#sec-embedding)
path goes through the lower-level eigensolve on the actual $(L, M)$ pair. If you
hand a cortical mesh's k-ring adjacency to `laplacian_eigenmap`, you get a
perfectly valid *graph* embedding — useful for connectivity-style analyses — but
its coordinates are metric-blind, so they will not match the analytic manifold
harmonics and will drift with triangulation density. Use the graph eigenmap for
graph questions; use the cotangent-mass eigensolve for geometry.

(`nitrix.graph` also exposes a `community` submodule — `modularity_matrix`,
`modularity_matrix_matvec`, `relaxed_modularity`, `coaffiliation`,
`girvan_newman_null` — whose operators are algebraically Laplacian-shaped but
serve *community detection*; they live apart from `laplacian.py` precisely to keep
the Laplacian import graph free of community-detection concerns. They are
mentioned here for completeness; their home is the connectivity unit named at the
end of this page.)

---

## k-ring operators and graph signal processing {#sec-gsp}

The bridge from "adjacency matrix" to "filter on a graph" is short. The k-ring
adjacency from [operators](operators.qmd#sec-adjacency) is built by
`mesh_k_ring_adjacency(mesh, k=, binary=, include_self=, format=)`
(**HOST-CTOR**, returns `ELL`/`SectionedELL`): a host-side BFS expands each
vertex's 1-ring to depth $k$, then packs the result into the chosen sparse format.
The two flags are the whole graph-operator vocabulary:

- `binary=True` (default): entries are $1$ for neighbours, $0$ (the ELL identity)
  for padding — **binary reachability**, the unweighted adjacency $A$.
- `binary=False`: entries are $1/|\mathcal{N}|$, **row-normalised** so the
  operator is row-stochastic — the **diffusion / averaging** operator $D^{-1}A$,
  the discrete random walk on the k-ring graph.
- `include_self=True` adds a self-loop (the vertex in its own neighbour list),
  turning a pure-neighbour aggregate into a "self + neighbours" aggregate (the
  GCN-style renormalisation trick).

Once you have such an operator, **mesh smoothing and boundary detection are graph
signal processing**: a per-vertex signal $u$ is a graph signal, and applying the
adjacency operator is a graph filter. Multiplying by the row-stochastic $D^{-1}A$
is a one-step **low-pass** filter (local averaging blurs high-frequency detail);
applying the graph Laplacian $L_{\text{graph}}=D-A$ is a **high-pass** filter that
amplifies where a signal disagrees with its neighbourhood — which is precisely a
discrete edge/boundary detector. Stacking these is "filtering on a graph": a
polynomial $p(L)$ in the Laplacian is a spectral filter (the ChebNet idea), and
the [semiring matvec](operators.qmd#sec-apply) is the engine that applies it. We
keep this a bridge, not a course; the GSP references at the end go deeper.

The deepest payoff of reading these operators as graph filters is that the
**same ELL substrate** carries all of them. The edge-functional primitive
`semiring_ell_edge_aggregate(edge_fn, ell, x, *, semiring)` (in
`nitrix.semiring`) runs a user callable $\varphi(h_i,h_j,w,(i,j))$ at every
(vertex, neighbour) pair and reduces per row under a chosen
[semiring](operators.qmd#sec-semiring) — REAL $(+,\times)$ for sum-aggregation
(GCN/EdgeConv/GAT), max-plus for symmetric pooling, min-plus for morphological
erosion. One primitive, the whole modern graph-conv family, no separate
message-passing API.

---

## Mesh pooling and hierarchies {#sec-pooling}

Surface-domain encoder–decoders (Topofit-, SUGAR-style networks on the cortex)
need **multi-resolution** representations: pool a fine mesh down to a coarse one,
process, then upsample back. On a regular grid that is ordinary CNN
pooling/unpooling; on a mesh the natural multi-resolution scaffold is the
**icosphere hierarchy**, because each subdivision step quadruples the faces in a
perfectly nested way.

### The icosphere hierarchy

`icosphere(n_iterations)` (**HOST-CTOR**) builds a geodesic sphere by recursively
subdividing an icosahedron: $0$ iterations gives the 12-vertex base, and each
subdivision takes a mesh with $n$ vertices to one with

$$
n_L = 10\cdot 4^{L} + 2
$$ {#eq-icosphere-count}

vertices at level $L$ — $12, 42, 162, 642, \dots$, with level $7$ matching
FreeSurfer's `ico7` at $163{,}842$ vertices. The geometric growth in
@eq-icosphere-count is the left panel of @fig-graph-geometry-hierarchy.

`icosphere_hierarchy(max_level)` (**HOST-CTOR**) returns an `IcosphereHierarchy`
dataclass holding `meshes[0..max_level]` *plus the parent-child bookkeeping*
needed to build cross-level operators in $O(n)$ without re-running subdivision.
The `parents[L]` table records, for each fine vertex, whether it is a
**coarse-original** (preserved in place: `parents[L][v] == (v, v)`) or the
**midpoint** of a coarse edge `(a, b)` (`parents[L][v] == (a, b)`). One important
as-built fact: `IcosphereHierarchy` is **deliberately not a JAX pytree** — its
parent tables are host-side construction metadata, and the object is a
*construction artefact* used once on the host to emit array-bearing meshes and
ELLs that *then* cross the jit/grad/vmap boundary. `icosphere_hierarchy_from_levels`
(**HOST-CTOR**) is the escape hatch: wrap your own (non-icosphere) meshes plus
parent tables into the same structure so every cross-level operator below works
unchanged.

### Cross-level operators are semiring matmuls in disguise

The hierarchy emits two cross-level ELLs:

- `icosphere_cross_level_adjacency(hierarchy, coarse_level, fine_level)`
  (**HOST-CTOR**, consecutive levels): row $i$ (coarse vertex) lists the fine
  vertices descending from it — the coarse-original child plus every edge-midpoint
  on an incident edge. Stored `values` are a $1/0$ validity indicator that serves
  both pooling consumers without a rebuild.
- `icosphere_bary_upsampler(hierarchy, coarse_level, fine_level)`
  (**HOST-CTOR**): row $i$ (fine vertex) lists its coarse sources with
  **barycentric weights** — weight $1$ on itself for a coarse-original, weights
  $(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$ on the two endpoints for an edge-midpoint.

The actual pooling/upsampling routines are **DIFF-JAX** and — this is the payoff
of [operators](operators.qmd#sec-semiring) — *each is no more than a semiring matmul across
levels*, packaged as a named wrapper for discoverability:

- `mesh_pool_max(cross_level_adjacency, fine_features)` — **max-plus** pool. For
  each coarse vertex, the max of its fine children's features. It is a
  `semiring_ell_matmul` under `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` with the stored values replaced
  by the max-plus identity (zeros), so the reduction degenerates to a plain `max`.
- `mesh_coarsen_meanpool(coarsen_ell, fine_features)` — **REAL** pool. The
  `values`-weighted mean of each coarse vertex's children:
  $\text{out}_i = \bigl(\sum_p \text{values}_{ip}\,\text{fine}_{\text{idx}_{ip}}\bigr) / \sum_p \text{values}_{ip}$.
  With the $1/0$ indicator this is a plain row-mean (padding drops out of both
  sum and count; an all-padding row returns zeros, not `NaN`). This is the
  scatter-mean-with-self-loop coarsening used by surface encoders such as SUGAR's
  `IcosahedronPooling`.
- `mesh_unpool_max(cross_level_adjacency, coarse_features)` — the max-plus
  *unpool*, symmetric to `mesh_pool_max` with the levels swapped; usually
  single-source per fine vertex, so the "max" reduces to a gather.
- `mesh_bary_upsample(bary_ell, coarse_coords)` — **REAL** matmul that
  barycentrically interpolates per-vertex coordinates/features from coarse to
  fine.

Because these share the `_apply_shared_ell` core, a single un-batched 2-D ELL
(one per level pair) broadcasts across batch/channel axes via `jax.vmap` without
materialising a batched copy of the operator. @fig-graph-geometry-hierarchy (right
panel) shows a signal max-pooled up a small hierarchy — the coarse levels keep the
*extremes* of the fine signal, which is the defining behaviour of max-pooling and
the reason it preserves salient features through a surface encoder.

```{python}
#| label: fig-graph-geometry-hierarchy
#| fig-cap: "Mesh hierarchies. Left: icosphere vertex count grows as n_L = 10·4^L + 2 (4x per subdivision). Right: a vertex signal max-pooled up a 3-level toy ring hierarchy built by the same coarse-original + edge-midpoint rule the real icosphere uses; each coarse vertex keeps the max over its children, so peaks survive coarsening (curves offset per level)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Panel A: icosphere vertex counts.
levels = np.arange(0, 8)
counts = 10 * 4 ** levels + 2

# Panel B: max-pool a vertex signal up a 3-level toy "icosphere stand-in".
# A ring of vertices refined by edge-midpoint subdivision (the same
# coarse-original + midpoint rule the real icosphere uses): coarse vertex i
# owns itself plus its two incident midpoints at the next finer level.
def subdivide_ring(n_coarse):
    children = []
    for i in range(n_coarse):
        mid_next = n_coarse + i                  # midpoint of edge (i, i+1)
        mid_prev = n_coarse + (i - 1) % n_coarse  # midpoint of edge (i-1, i)
        children.append([i, mid_next, mid_prev])
    return 2 * n_coarse, children

def max_pool(children, fine_signal):  # mesh_pool_max: max over children
    return np.array([fine_signal[ch].max() for ch in children])

n0 = 12
n1, ch01 = subdivide_ring(n0)
n2, ch12 = subdivide_ring(n1)

ang2 = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, n2, endpoint=False)
sig2 = np.sin(3 * ang2) + 0.4 * np.cos(7 * ang2)  # bumpy analytic signal
sig1 = max_pool(ch12, sig2)   # pool 2 -> 1
sig0 = max_pool(ch01, sig1)   # pool 1 -> 0

fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4))

axs[0].plot(levels, counts, "o-", color="crimson")
axs[0].set_yscale("log")
axs[0].set_xlabel("subdivision level $L$")
axs[0].set_ylabel("vertices  $n_L = 10\\cdot 4^{L}+2$")
axs[0].set_title("Icosphere hierarchy: 4x growth per level")
for L in (0, 3, 6):
    axs[0].annotate(f"{counts[L]}", (L, counts[L]), textcoords="offset points",
                    xytext=(6, -2), fontsize=8)
axs[0].grid(True, which="both", alpha=0.3)

ax = axs[1]
for sig, lab, off in ((sig2, "level 2 (fine)", 0.0),
                      (sig1, "level 1", 3.2),
                      (sig0, "level 0 (coarse)", 6.4)):
    a = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, len(sig), endpoint=False)
    ax.plot(a, sig + off, "o-", ms=3, label=lab)
ax.set_xlabel("angle around ring")
ax.set_ylabel("max-pooled signal (offset per level)")
ax.set_title("Max-plus pooling up a mesh hierarchy")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The volumetric counterpart

For volumes the analogue is SegNet-style **indexed** pooling, in
`nitrix.morphology.pooling`:

- `max_pool_with_indices_nd(x, pool_size=, spatial_rank=)` (**DIFF-JAX**):
  channel-first N-D max pooling that returns both the pooled values *and* the
  flattened argmax index of each window's max in the original grid. The
  implementation "unfolds" each spatial axis into (window-count, window-size),
  takes `jnp.argmax` over the flattened window axes, and combines the
  within-window coordinate with the window's anchor to get a global flat index —
  $O(N)$ in voxels, no per-window scan. Spatial dims must be divisible by
  `pool_size` (the caller pre-pads).
- `max_unpool_nd(x, indices, output_shape=, spatial_rank=)` (**DIFF-JAX**):
  scatters pooled values back to a higher-resolution grid *at the stored argmax
  positions*, zeros elsewhere — the exact inverse-routing SegNet's decoder uses to
  place each pooled value where it came from.

Ties resolve to the first argmax (lowest within-window flat index), matching
`jnp.argmax`; cross-framework consumers should expect tie-breaking to differ —
the unit's "validate by class/correlation, not bit-parity" posture again.

Together — the cross-level mesh wrappers, the icosphere hierarchy that builds
their ELLs, the edge-functional aggregate, and the indexed volumetric pooling —
these close the loop for **geometric deep learning on surfaces**: a coherent
encoder–decoder stack expressed entirely in the semiring/ELL substrate.

---

## Pointers onward

This page is where the geometry unit hands off to the federation's
**connectivity, parcellation, and labels** work, which builds directly on the
graph view above but is a *separate unit*. `nitrix.graph.parcellation` already
ships the functional-parcellation pipeline — `surface_boundary_map`
(connectivity-profile boundary detection composing
`semiring_ell_edge_aggregate`), `eta_squared` similarity, and `mesh_watershed`
(host-side priority-flood basins on a per-vertex field) — and `nitrix.graph.community`
the modularity operators. Documenting connectopy (`laplacian_eigenmap` /
`diffusion_embedding` as connectivity *gradients*), parcellation (boundary-map →
watershed), and community detection in full is the job of that unit; here we only
needed the graph-Laplacian / spectral / pooling machinery they stand on. See the
package at [`nitrix.graph`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/graph/).

---

## Further reading

- Chung, F. R. K. (1997). *Spectral Graph Theory*. CBMS Regional Conference
  Series in Mathematics 92, American Mathematical Society. (The canonical
  treatment of the graph Laplacian and its spectrum.)
- Belkin, M., & Niyogi, P. (2003). "Laplacian Eigenmaps for Dimensionality
  Reduction and Data Representation." *Neural Computation*, 15(6), 1373–1396.
  (The eigenmap embedding `laplacian_eigenmap` implements.)
- Coifman, R. R., & Lafon, S. (2006). "Diffusion maps." *Applied and
  Computational Harmonic Analysis*, 21(1), 5–30. (The basis of
  `diffusion_embedding`.)
- Ortega, A., Frossard, P., Kovačević, J., Moura, J. M. F., & Vandergheynst, P.
  (2018). "Graph Signal Processing: Overview, Challenges, and Applications."
  *Proceedings of the IEEE*, 106(5), 808–828.
- Bronstein, M. M., Bruna, J., Cohen, T., & Veličković, P. (2021). *Geometric
  Deep Learning: Grids, Groups, Graphs, Geodesics, and Gauges*. arXiv:2104.13478.
- Defferrard, M., Bresson, X., & Vandergheynst, P. (2016). "Convolutional Neural
  Networks on Graphs with Fast Localized Spectral Filtering" (ChebNet).
  *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems* 29. (Polynomial spectral
  filters $p(L)$.)
- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). "Discrete
  Differential-Geometry Operators for Triangulated 2-Manifolds." In *Visualization
  and Mathematics III*, 35–57. (The cotangent Laplacian and mixed-Voronoi mass.)

---

## See also

- Source: [`graph/connectopy.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/graph/connectopy.py)
  — `laplacian_eigenmap`, `diffusion_embedding`.
- Source: [`graph/laplacian.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/graph/laplacian.py)
  — `laplacian`, `laplacian_matvec`, `degree_vector`, `in_degree_vector`,
  `symmetric_degree_vector`.
- Source: [`sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
  — `mesh_k_ring_adjacency`, `mesh_laplacian_smooth`, the icosphere hierarchy
  (`icosphere`, `icosphere_hierarchy`, `icosphere_hierarchy_from_levels`,
  `icosphere_cross_level_adjacency`, `icosphere_bary_upsampler`), and the
  cross-level wrappers (`mesh_pool_max`, `mesh_coarsen_meanpool`,
  `mesh_unpool_max`, `mesh_bary_upsample`).
- Source: [`morphology/pooling.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/pooling.py)
  — `max_pool_with_indices_nd`, `max_unpool_nd`.
- Design: [`docs/design/mesh-graph-conv.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/mesh-graph-conv.md)
  — the edge-functional substrate and the mesh-graph CNN stack.
- Neighbouring pages: [operators](operators.qmd#sec-adjacency) (k-ring adjacency,
  ELL, semirings), [laplace-beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent)
  (cotangent Laplacian, mass), [spectral](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen) (manifold
  harmonics), [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve) (the eigensolve dispatcher),
  [volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-morphology) (volumetric morphology).
